questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), find the value of {tex}\angle{/tex}DOC.
questions.explanation:
We know that, angle made on the straight line is 180°.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3x + 4x + 2x = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOF = 3x = 60°
As,{tex}\angle{/tex}DOC = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOF (vertically opposite angles)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DOC = 60°

questions.question: If the angles of a triangle are in the ratio 3 : 4 : 5, then the triangle formed will be
questions.explanation:
Let angles of the triangle be 3x, 4x , 5x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3x + 4x + 5x = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required angles are 45°, 60°, 75°
So, the triangle formed will be scalene triangle.

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), if CE II BA, then the value of {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB is
questions.explanation:
Since, CE || BA and AC is transversal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACE = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 60° (alternate interior angle)
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACE + {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 60° + 65° = 125°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 180° - 125° = 55°

questions.question: In figure (not drawn to scale), if AB || CD, CD || EF and y : z = 4:5, then find the value of x.
questions.explanation:
Given that AB || CD and CD || EF
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB || CD || EF
x + y = 180° ...(i) (co-interior angles)
y + z = 180° ...(ii) [ZDHl - y, (vertically opposite angles)]
Given that y : z = 4 :5 {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{4}{5} z{/tex}
Substituting in (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{4 z}{5}+z=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow \frac{9 z}{5}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow z=\frac{180^{\circ} \times 5}{9} \Rightarrow z=100^{\circ}{/tex}
y + 100° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 80°
From (i), we have
x + 80° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 100°

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), AB || CD and PQ, QR intersects AB and CD both at E, F and G, FI respectively. Find the value of x.
questions.explanation:
In the given figure, AB || CD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PEB = {tex}\angle{/tex}PGD = 80° (corresponding angles)
Now, EGQ is a straight line.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EGD + {tex}\angle{/tex}DGQ = 180° (linear pair)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DGQ = 100°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}QHD = x + {tex}\angle{/tex}DGQ (exterior angle property)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 120° = x + 100° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20°

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), AB || CD and AB || EF. If EA {tex}\perp{/tex} BA and {tex}\angle{/tex}BEF = 55°, then the values of x, y and z respectively are_______.
questions.explanation:
We are given, AB || CD and AB || EF
So, CD || EF
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 55° + x = 180° (co-interior angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 180° - 55° = 125°
Also, AB || CD, so
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y = 125° (corresponding angles)
Now, as AB || EF
{tex}\angle{/tex}AEF + {tex}\angle{/tex}EAB = 180° (co-interior angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z + 55°+ 90° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 180°-145° = 35°

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), {tex}\angle{/tex}APO = 42° and {tex}\angle{/tex}CQO = 38°. Find the value of {tex}\angle{/tex}POQ.
questions.explanation:
It is given that,
AB || MN || CD
and {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = 42°, {tex}\angle{/tex}4 = 38°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 (Alternate angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 42°
And {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 (Alternate angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = 38°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}POQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 + {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = 42° + 38° = 80°

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), lines XY and MN intersect at O. lf {tex}\angle{/tex}POY=90°and a : b = 2 : 3, then {tex}\angle{/tex}XON is equal to ________.
questions.explanation:
Let a = 2x and b = 3x
Now, XOY is a straight line.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2x + 3x + 90° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x = 90° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 18°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}XON = {tex}\angle{/tex}MOY = {tex}\angle{/tex}MOP + {tex}\angle{/tex}POY (vertically opposite angles)
= 36°+ 90° = 126°.

questions.question: The value of x, in the given triangle is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\angle{/tex}P + {tex}\angle{/tex}Q + {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 180° (Angle sum property)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x + 8° + 55° + 8x - 3° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20x + 60° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20x = 120° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6°

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), PQ || RS and EF || {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS. If = 55°, then the measure of {tex}\angle{/tex}RFE is
questions.explanation:
Since PQ || RS
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS + {tex}\angle{/tex}RSQ = 180° (Co-interior angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 55° + {tex}\angle{/tex}RSQ = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RSQ =125°
Now, EF || QS
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RFE = {tex}\angle{/tex}RSQ = 125° (Corresponding angles)

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), AB || DC. Then the value of x is ________.
questions.explanation:
Through E, draw a line GEH such that GH || AB || CD.

As, GE || AB and AE is transversal
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}GEA = {tex}\angle{/tex}EAB = 50° (Alternate angles)
Also, EH || CD and EC is transversal
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}HEC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 180° (Co-interior angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}HEC = 80°
As, GEH is a straight line
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}GEA + {tex}\angle{/tex}AEC + {tex}\angle{/tex}HEC = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50° + x + 80° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 180° - 130° = 50°

questions.question: Two angles measure (25° - a) and (135° + 2a). If each one is the supplement of the other, then the value of a is
In the given figure (not drawn to scale), AB || CD. Find the value of x.
questions.explanation:
Draw EO || AB || CD

So, {tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = x
As EO || AB and OB is transversal
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABO = 180° (co-interior angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 +40° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = 140°
Also, EO || CD and DO is transversal
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}2 + {tex}\angle{/tex}CDO = 180° (co-interior angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}2 + 35° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 145°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 140° + 145° = 285°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 285°

questions.question: If two complementary angles are in the ratio 7 : 11, then the angles are ________.
questions.explanation:
Let the angles be 7x and 11x
Now, the given angles are complementary
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 7x + 11x = 90° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 18x = 90° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} First angle = 35°
And second angle = 55°.

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), if AB || CD, then x and y respectively are ________.
questions.explanation:
Since AB || CD
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\angle{/tex}APQ = 50° (alternate angles)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, x + y = 127° (exterior angle property)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 127°-50°{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 77°

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), if OCD is an isosceles triangle in which OD and OC are equal, then what will be the value of {tex}\angle{/tex}OCD?
questions.explanation:
x + 40° + x + x + 20° = 180° (Linear pair)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 60° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 120° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 40°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}OCD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ODC [{tex}\because{/tex} OD = OC]
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}C + {tex}\angle{/tex}D + x = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OCD + {tex}\angle{/tex}ODC + 40° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}OCD = 140° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OCD = 70°

questions.question: In the given figure (not drawn to scale), the value of y is ________.
questions.explanation:
We have,
{tex}\angle{/tex}AOF = {tex}\angle{/tex}COD = 5y (vertically opposite angles)
Now, BOE is a straight line.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 y + 5 y + y = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9y = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 20°

questions.question:
Use the given figure to match Column-I with Column-ll, if AB || CD.
_

Column-I Column-II
(a) Corresponding angles (i) {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}7
(b) Alternate interior angles (ii) {tex}\angle{/tex}4 + {tex}\angle{/tex}5 = 180°
(c) Alternate exterior angles (iii) {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}5
(d) Co-interior angles (iv) {tex}\angle{/tex}4 = {tex}\angle{/tex}6
questions.explanation:
(a) Corresponding angles {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}5
(b) Alternate interior angles{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}4 = {tex}\angle{/tex}6
(c) Alternate exterior angles {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}7
(d) Co-interior angles {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}4 + {tex}\angle{/tex}5 = 180°

questions.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT?

options.option: Bisectors of the adjacent angles forming a linear pair form a right angle.
If two angles forming a linear pair, then each of these angle is of measure 90°.
Both of the angles forming a linear pair can be obtuse angles.
Angles forming a linear pair can both be acute angles.

questions.explanation: Bisectors of the adjacent angles forming a linear pair form a right angle.

questions.question: IF AB {tex}\bot{/tex} BC and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BCA, then which of the following statements is true?
questions.explanation: ABC is a right-angled isosceles triangle with  {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC ={tex}\angle{/tex}BCA and hence sides opposite to equal angles must be equal. Hence, we can say that AB = BC.

questions.question: If {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC is an isosceles triangle with AB = AC and {tex}\angle {/tex}B = 65o, find {tex}\angle {/tex}A.
questions.explanation:
In isosceles triangle ABC
AB = AC (Given)
Therefore {tex}\angle {/tex} B ={tex}\angle {/tex} C = 65o  (angles opposite to equal side are equal).
So, by applying angle sum property i.e {tex}\angle {/tex} A+{tex}\angle {/tex} B+{tex}\angle {/tex} C=180o,
{tex}\angle {/tex} A + 65o + 65o = 180o
{tex}\angle {/tex} A = 180o - 130o
{tex}\angle {/tex}A = 50o

questions.question: What is the sum of the angles of a quadrilateral?
questions.explanation:
For a quadrilateral
Number of sides (n) = 4
Sum of interior angles = (n - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 180°
p = (4 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 180°
p = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 180°
p = 360°

questions.question: The sum of the interior angles of a triangle is:
questions.explanation:
For  a triangle,
Number of sides (n) = 3
Sum of interior angles = (n - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 180o
= (3 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 180o
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 180o
= 180o

questions.question: Find the measure of angles which is equal to its supplement.
questions.explanation:
We know that the sum of supplementary angle is 180o
Let the  angle be x the other angle is 180o - x and  the two angles are equal,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 180o - x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 180o 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{180^{\circ}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 90o

questions.question: Find the measure of each exterior angle of an equilateral triangle.
questions.explanation:
We know that in equilateral triangle each angle is 60o
and we know sum of interior angle and exterior angle is 180o
let exterior angle be x
60o + x = 180o
x = 180o - 60o
x = 120o

questions.question: In an isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, if AB = AC and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90o, Find {tex}\angle{/tex}B.
questions.explanation:
We know that sum of all angles of a triangle is 180o
so {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180o|
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90o 
AB = AC
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C ( The angle opposite to equal side is also equal)
90o + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180o - 90o
{tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 90o ( {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C)
2{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 90o
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\frac{{90^ \circ }}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 45o ={tex}\angle{/tex}C

questions.question: In a {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, if {tex}\angle{/tex} B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 45o, Which is the longest side?
questions.explanation:
We know that sum of all angles of a triangle is 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 45o
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + 45o + 45o = 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}A +  90o = 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 180o - 90o
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90o
So, angle A is the largest and the side opposite to the greatest angle is the longest so, side BC is the longest.

questions.question: A triangle that has no equal sides and no equal angles is known as
questions.explanation: scalene triangle

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex}, {tex}\angle C{/tex} = {tex}\angle A{/tex} and BC = 6 cm and AC = 5 cm. Then the length of AB is:
questions.explanation: Sides opposite to equal angles are equal.Since,{tex}\angle C{/tex}  = {tex}\angle A{/tex} , hence , AB=BC = 6 cm

questions.question: The cost of turfing a triangular field at the rate of Rs. 45 per 100 m2 is Rs. 900. If the double the base of the triangle is 5 times its height, then its height is
questions.explanation:
Cost of turfing a triangular field at the rate of Rs.45 per 100 = Rs.900
{tex}{{{\rm{Area }} \times 45} \over {100}} = 900{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Area = 2000 sq. m
According to question,
2 {tex}\times{/tex} Base = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} Height
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Base = {tex}{{{\rm{Height }} \times 5} \over 2}{/tex}
Area of triangle = 2000 sq. m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height = 2000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}{{{\rm{Height }} \times 5} \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Height = 2000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (Height)2 = 1600
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Height = 40 m

questions.question: If {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC{tex}\cong \triangle{/tex} PQR and {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC is not congruent to {tex}\triangle{/tex} RPQ, then which of the following is not true:
questions.explanation:
According to the condition given in the question,
If {tex}\triangle A B C \cong \triangle P Q R{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} is not congruent to {tex}\triangle R P Q{/tex}
Then, clearly BC {tex}\neq{/tex} PQ
{tex}\therefore{/tex} It is false

questions.question: It is given that {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong \triangle{/tex}FDE and AB = 5 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 40° and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 80°. Then which of the following is true?
questions.explanation:
Given that: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, AB = 5 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 40° and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 80°
Using angles sum property of triangle, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 80° + 40° + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 120° + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°  [{tex}\because \angle{/tex}B = 40° and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 80° ]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ∠C = 180° – 120°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ∠C = 60°
It is given that {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong\triangle{/tex}FDE, so we have
AB = FD, BC = DE and AC = FE & {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}F, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}D and {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}E
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = FD = 5cm and {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}E = 60°.

questions.question: If {tex}\triangle A B C \cong \triangle L K M{/tex}, then side of {tex}\triangle{/tex}LKM equal to side AC of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is
questions.explanation: Since, by corresponding part of congruent triangle AC of {tex}\triangle {/tex}ABC is equal to the LM of {tex}\triangle{/tex}LKM.

questions.question: In triangles ABC and PQR, if {tex}\angle A=\angle R, \angle B=\angle P{/tex} and AB = RP, then which one of the following congruence conditions applies:
questions.explanation: Since, the two adjacent angles and the contained side are shown equal while proving the two triangles congruent. Hence, by A.S.A. theorem the two triangles can be proved congruent.

questions.question: If {tex}\Delta P Q R \cong \Delta E F D{/tex}, then ED =
questions.explanation: Since, by corresponding part of congruent triangle ED of {tex}\triangle{/tex}EFD is equal to the PR of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR.

questions.question: If {tex}\triangle P Q R \equiv \triangle E F D{/tex}, then {tex}\angle E={/tex}
questions.explanation: Since, by corresponding part of congruent {tex}\angle E{/tex} of {tex}\triangle{/tex}EFD is equal to the {tex}\angle P{/tex} of {tex}\triangle {/tex}PQR.

questions.question: In an isosceles triangle, if the vertex angle is twice the sum of the base angles, then the measure of vertex angle of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Let the base angles be x each,
Vertex angle = 2(x + x) = 4x
Now, since the sum of all the angles of a triangle​ is 180°
x + x + 4x = 180°
6x = 180°
x = 30°
Therefore, vertex angle= 4x = 120°

questions.question: If all the three angles of a triangle are equal, then each one of them is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let the measure of each angle be x°.
Now, the sum of all angles of any triangle is 180°.
Thus, x° + x° + x° = 180°
i.e. 3x° = 180°
i.e. x° = 60°

questions.question: If two acute angles of a right triangle are equal, then each acute is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let the measure of each acute angle of a triangle be x°.
Then, we have
x° + x° + 90° = 180°
i.e. 2x° = 90°
i.e. x° = 45°

questions.question: An exterior angle of a triangle is equal to 100° and two interior opposite angles are equal. Each of these angles is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let the two interior opposite angles be x° each.
Now, the exterior angle is equal to the sum of the two interior opposite angles.
x° + x° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 x^{\circ}=100^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{0}=50^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: Side BC of a triangle ABC has been produced to a point D such that {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 120°. If {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A, then {tex}\angle{/tex}A is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACD is an exterior angle
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 120°
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3 \angle A}{2}{/tex} = 120°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 240°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 80°

questions.question: In a triangle, an exterior angle at a vertex is 95° and its one of the interior opposite angle is 55°, then the measure of the other interior angle is
questions.explanation:
Let the other interior opposite angle be x°.
Then, we have x° +  55° = 95°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x°= 95°- 55° = 40°

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C and ray AX bisects the exterior angle {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC. If {tex}\angle{/tex}DAX = 70°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB =
questions.explanation:
AX is bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle D A X=\angle X A C=70^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle D A C=2 \times 70=140^{\circ}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC = 180°c- 180° = 40°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC 
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow 40^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C= 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow 40^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}\angle{/tex}C + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180° ...({tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle C{/tex} = 140°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle C{/tex} = 70°
i.e. {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 70°

questions.question: If the sides of a triangle are produced in order, then the sum of the three exterior angles so formed is
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}FAB = 180°- {tex}\angle{/tex}A ...(i)
{tex}\angle{/tex}DCA = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}C ...(ii)
{tex}\angle{/tex}EBC = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}B ...(iii) 
Adding equations (i), (ii) and (iii)
{tex}\angle{/tex}FAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}DCA + {tex}\angle{/tex}EBC = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}A + 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}C + 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}B
= 540° - ({tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C)
= 540° - 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Sum of All exterior angles = 360°

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, if {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 100°, AD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A and AD {tex}\perp {/tex} BC. Then, {tex}\angle{/tex}B =
questions.explanation:
AD {tex}\perp {/tex} BC an AD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD = 50°
In Right {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = 50°, {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 90°
Also sum of all interior angles = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}BAD +{tex}\angle{/tex}ADB + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}B = 180° - 50° - 90°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}B = 40°

questions.question: An exterior angle of a triangle is 108° and its interior opposite angles are in the ratio 4 : 5. The angles of the triangle are.
questions.explanation:
From figure, we have
{tex}\angle {/tex}A + {tex}\angle {/tex}B = {tex}\angle {/tex}ACD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x° + 5x° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x° = 108°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 12°
So, {tex}\angle {/tex}A = 48°, {tex}\angle {/tex}B = 60°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle {/tex}C = 180° - 48° - 60° = 72°

questions.question: In a {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC,if {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 60°, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 80° and the bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}B and {tex}\angle{/tex}C meet at O, the {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC =
questions.explanation:
o is point where bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}C & {tex}\angle{/tex}B meets.
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
60° + 80° + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}C = 40°
{tex}\frac{\angle C}{2}{/tex} = 20°
{tex}\frac{\angle C}{2}{/tex} = 20° = {tex}\angle{/tex}BCO ...(i)
{tex}\frac{\angle B}{2}{/tex}  = {tex}\frac{80^{\circ}}{2}{/tex} = 40° = {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC ...(ii)
In {tex}\triangle B O C{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}BCO + {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180°
From (i) and (ii)
20° + 40° + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B O C{/tex} = 180° - 60° = 120°

questions.question: Line sgements AB and CD intersect at O such that AC||DB. If {tex}\angle{/tex}CAB = 45° and {tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = 55°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}BOD =
questions.explanation:
AC||BD
And, AB is transverse to these parallel lines
So {tex}\angle{/tex}CAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD (Alternate angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}ABD = 45°
Now In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOD
{tex}\angle{/tex}BOD + {tex}\angle{/tex}ODB + {tex}\angle{/tex}DBA = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}DBA = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 45°, {tex}\angle{/tex}ODB = 55°
So {tex}\angle{/tex}BOD = 180° - 45° - 55°
= 80°

questions.question: If the measures of angles of a triangle are in the ratio of 3 : 4 : 5, what is the measure of the smallest angle of the triangle?
questions.explanation:
The measures of angles of a triangle are in ratio 3: 4: 5.
Let the angles be 3x, 4x and 5x.
In any triangle, sum of all angles = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 4x + 5x = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15°
So, smallest angle = 3 {tex}\times 15^{\circ}{/tex} = 45°

questions.question: If the bisectors of the acute angles of a right triangle meet at O, then the angle at O between the two bisectors is
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex},
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + 90° + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 90° ...(i)
Now, in {tex}\triangle \mathrm{AOC}{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}COA + {tex}\angle{/tex}OAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}OCA = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{COA}+\frac{\angle \mathrm{A}}{2}+\frac{\angle \mathrm{C}}{2}{/tex} = 180° {AO and CO bisects angle {tex}\angle{/tex}A and {tex}\angle{/tex}C}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}COA = 180 - {tex}\left(\frac{\angle \mathrm{A}+\angle \mathrm{C}}{2}\right){/tex}
= 180° - {tex}\left(\frac{90^{\circ}}{2}\right){/tex}{from (i)}
= 180° - 45° = 135°

questions.question: In a {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex}, if {tex}\angle A-\angle B=42^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\angle B-\angle C=21^{\circ}{/tex} then {tex}\angle B{/tex} = ?
questions.explanation:
Let 
{tex}\angle A-\angle B=42^{\circ}{/tex}......(i) and 
{tex}\angle B-\angle C=21^{\circ}{/tex} ....(ii) 
Adding (i) and (ii),we get 
{tex}\angle A-\angle C=63^{\circ}{/tex}  .... (iii)
{tex}\angle B=\angle A-42^{\circ} {/tex} ......[using (i)]
{tex}\angle C=\angle A-63^{\circ}{/tex} [Using (iii)]
{tex}\therefore \angle A+\angle B+\angle C=180^{\circ}{/tex}  [Sum of the angles of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A+\angle A-42^{\circ}+\angle A-63^{\circ}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \angle A-105^{\circ}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \angle A=285^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \angle B=(95-42)^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B=53^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: The base BC of triangle ABC is produced both ways and the measure of exterior angles formed are 94° and 126°. Then, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC =
questions.explanation:
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180° - 126° = 54°
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 180° - 94° = 86°
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 180^{\circ}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}BAC = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC - {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB
= 180° - 54°  - 86°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}BAC = 40°

questions.question: In triangles ABC and DEF, AB = FD and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}D. The two triangles will be congruent by SAS axiom if
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF, AB = FD and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}D

The two triangles will be congruent by SAS axiom if two sides including one angle of one triangle is equal to the another triangle.
Hence, AC = DE (As {tex}\angle{/tex}A is between AB and AC)

questions.question: In triangles ABC and PQR, AB = AC, {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}P and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q. The two triangles are
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, AB = AC, {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}P and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q

AB = AC
{tex}\Rightarrow\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C (opposite angles to equal sides are equal)
Hence, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle.
{tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}P and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}P = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q ({tex}\therefore\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} QR = PR (opposite sides to equal angles are equal)
Hence, {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is an isosceles triangle.
So, the two triangles are isosceles but not congruent.
As AAA is not the criterion for a triangle to be congruent.

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, BC = AB and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 80°. Then {tex}\angle{/tex}A is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, BC = AB and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 80o

As BC = AB
So it is an isosceles triangle.
let {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}A = x
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 80° (given)
As we know {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 80° + x = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 180° - 80°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 100°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 50°
So, ∠C = ∠A = 50°

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, AB = AC and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 50°. Then {tex}\angle{/tex}C is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, AB = AC and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 50°.

As AB = AC
So it is an isosceles triangle.
So {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 50° (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow\angle{/tex}C = 50°

questions.question: In a {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex}, if {tex}3 \angle A = 4 \angle B=6 \angle C{/tex} then A : B : C = ?
questions.explanation:
3A = 4B = 6C
3A = 4B ⇒ A/B = 4/3
This means, A:B=4:3
4B = 6C ⇒ B/C = 6/4 = 3/2
⇒ B:C = 3:2
The value of B in 4:3 is equal to the value of B in 3:2
Hence A : B : C = 4 : 3 : 2

questions.question: The bisector of exterior angles at B and C of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC meet at O. If {tex}\angle{/tex}A = x°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC =
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex},
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}180-x^{0}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\angle{/tex}CBD = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\angle{/tex}B ...(ii)
{tex}\angle{/tex}ECB = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\angle{/tex}C ...(iii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\angle \mathrm{CBD}}{2}=\angle \mathrm{OBC}{/tex} = {tex}90^{\circ}-\frac{\angle B}{2}{/tex} ...(iv) [From eq (ii)]
{tex}\frac{\angle \mathrm{ECB}}{2}=\angle \mathrm{OCB}{/tex} = {tex}90^{\circ}-\frac{\angle \mathrm{C}}{2}{/tex} ...(v) [From eq (iii)]
Now, in {tex}\triangle BOC{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}OBC + {tex}\angle{/tex}OCB + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
 {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - ({tex}\angle{/tex}OBC + {tex}\angle{/tex}OCB)
From eq (iv) and (v)
 {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - ({tex}90^{\circ}-\frac{\angle B}{2}+90^{\circ}-\frac{\angle C}{2}{/tex})
= {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}- ({tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\angle B}{2}-\frac{\angle C}{2}{/tex})
= {tex}\frac{\angle B+\angle C}{2}{/tex} [from eq (i)]
= {tex}\frac{180^{\circ}-x^{\circ}}{2}{/tex}
 {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = {tex}90^{\circ}-\frac{x^{\circ}}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: In a {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 50° and BC is produced to a point D. If the bisectors {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD meet at E, then {tex}\angle{/tex}E =
questions.explanation:
BE and CE are bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}B and {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}50^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}130^{\circ}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}BEC
{tex}\angle{/tex}CBE + {tex}\angle{/tex}BEC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ECB = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = {tex}\frac{\angle \mathrm{B}}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\angle{/tex}BEC = {tex}\angle{/tex}E, {tex}\angle{/tex}ECB = {tex}\angle{/tex}C + {tex}\angle{/tex}ACE
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 180^{\circ}- {tex}\angle{/tex}C
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACE = {tex}\frac{\angle \mathrm{ACD}}{2}=\frac{180^{\circ}-\angle \mathrm{C}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}90^{\circ}-\frac{\angle \mathrm{C}}{2}{/tex}
So. {tex}\angle{/tex}ECB = {tex}\angle{/tex}C + {tex}90^{\circ}-\frac{\angle C}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex} ECB = {tex}90^{\circ}+\frac{\angle C}{2}{/tex}
Now putting all values in eq (ii)
{tex}\frac{\angle B}{2}+\angle E+90^{\circ}+\frac{\angle C}{2}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}E = {tex}180^{\circ}-90^{\circ}-\left(\frac{\angle \mathrm{B}+\angle \mathrm{C}}{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}90^{\circ}-\left(\frac{\angle \mathrm{B}+\angle \mathrm{C}}{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}90^{\circ}-\frac{130^{\circ}}{2}{/tex} [From eq (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}E = {tex}25^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: The side BC of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is produced to a point D. The bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}A meets side in L. If {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 30° and {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 115°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}ALC =
questions.explanation:
{tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 180° - 115° = 65°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex}A = 180 - 30° - 65°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle{/tex} A = 85°
Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ALC
{tex}\angle{/tex}ALC + {tex}\angle{/tex}LAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle {/tex}ALC = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}LAC - {tex}\angle{/tex}C
= {tex}180^{\circ}-\frac{\angle \mathrm{A}}{2}-\angle \mathrm{C} {/tex}
= {tex}180^{\circ}-\frac{85^{\circ}}{2}-65^{\circ} {/tex}
= {tex}\frac{145^{\circ}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}72 \frac{1}{2}^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{PQR}{/tex}, if {tex}\angle \mathrm{R}>\angle \mathrm{Q},{/tex} then
questions.explanation: If in a triangle two angles are not equal, then the side opposite to the greater angle is longer than the side opposite to the smaller angle.

questions.question: Two sides of a triangle are of length 4 cm and 2.5 cm. The length of the third side of the triangle cannot be
questions.explanation: Length of the greatest side of a triangle must be less than the sum of the other two sides.

questions.question: In {tex}\Delta A B C, \text { if } \angle C>\angle B{/tex}  then
questions.explanation: Greater angle has greater side opposite to it.

questions.question: in {tex}\Delta A B C \text { and } \Delta D E F{/tex} it is given that AB = DE and BC = EF in order that {tex}\Delta A B C \cong \Delta D E F,{/tex} we must have
questions.explanation:
Given, AB = DE and BC = EF
for, {tex}\angle B=\angle E{/tex}
{tex}\Delta A B C \cong \Delta D E F{/tex}   [SAS]

questions.question: In {tex}\Delta A B C \text { and } \Delta D E F{/tex} its is given that {tex}\angle B=\angle E \text { and } \angle C=\angle F{/tex}  in order that {tex}\Delta A B C \cong \Delta D E F{/tex} we must have
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\Delta A B C \text { and } \Delta D E F{/tex}
{tex}\angle B=\angle E \text { and } \angle C=\angle F{/tex}
For congruence, BC = EF 
Therefore by AAS axiom
{tex}\Delta A B C \cong \Delta D E F{/tex}

questions.question: If the altitudes from two vertices of a triangle to the opposite sides are equal then the triangle is
questions.explanation:
In triangles ABE and ACF
{tex}\angle \mathrm{AEB}=\angle \mathrm{AFC}{/tex} (90o each)
{tex}\angle \mathrm{BAE}=\angle \mathrm{CAF}{/tex} (common angle)
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{ABE}=\angle \mathrm{ACF}{/tex} ... using angle sum property
BE = CF (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{ABE} \cong \Delta \mathrm{ACF}{/tex} (ASA)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = AC (c.p.c.t)
Hence, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle.... as two sides are equal to each other.

questions.question: In {tex}\Delta A B C, A B=A C \text { and } \angle B=50^{\circ}{/tex} Then {tex}\angle A=?{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
AB = AC and {tex}\angle B{/tex} = 50°
therefore, {tex}\angle C{/tex} = 50° also [angles opposite to equal sides are equal ]
{tex}\angle A+ \angle B+ \angle C{/tex} = 180° [by angle sum property of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A + 50° + 50° = 180°{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A{/tex} = 80°

questions.question: In {tex}\Delta A B C, \angle A=40^{\circ} \text { and } \angle B=60^{\circ}{/tex} Then, the longest side of {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
The third angle {tex}\angle C{/tex} = 80° [ Angle sum property ]
The side opposite to the longest angle is the longest side.
Therefore, AB is the longest side of the triangle.

questions.explanation: From above figure, {tex}\triangle \mathrm{CBA} \cong \triangle \mathrm{PRQ}{/tex}

questions.question: Which rule is applied to prove that congruency of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PRT.
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PRT
PQ = PR (Given)
QS = TR (Given)
{tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\triangle{/tex}PRQ (corresponding angles of an isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex})
By SAS congruency
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PRT

questions.question: In RHS rule H stands for:
questions.explanation: H stands for the hypotenuse.

questions.explanation: An isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex} has 2 sides equal.

questions.question: If PQ = 6 cm and QR = 7 cm, then perimeter of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is:
questions.explanation:
Perimeter = sum of all 3 sides
PQ = PR = 6 cm,
QR = 7 cm
So, P = (6 + 6 + 7) cm
= 19 cm

questions.question: If {tex}\angle{/tex}QPR = 80o find {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR?
questions.explanation:
let {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = {tex}\angle{/tex}R = x and {tex}\angle{/tex}P = 80o
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, {tex}\angle{/tex}P + {tex}\angle{/tex}Q + {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 180o (Angle sum property of {tex}\triangle{/tex})
80o + x + x = 180o
2x = 180o - 80
2x = 100o
x = {tex}\frac{100^{\circ}}{2}{/tex}
= 50o

questions.question: What is the shape of quadrilateral BEFD?
questions.explanation:
Since D, E, F are mid-points of AB, BC and CA respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} by mid-point theorem.
DF||BC and EF|| AB
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} DF||BE and EF||BD
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BEFD is a||gm.

questions.question: Area of portion II =
questions.explanation:
Since the diagonal of a|Igm divide it into two congruent triangles.
 {tex}\therefore \triangle \mathrm{DEF} \cong \triangle \mathrm{BED}{/tex} 
Similarly {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF a {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADF and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF a {tex}\triangle{/tex}CEF
{tex}\therefore \triangle{/tex}DEF {tex}\cong \triangle{/tex}BED {tex}\cong \triangle{/tex} ADF {tex}\cong \triangle{/tex}CEF
{tex}\therefore{/tex}area of all these triangles are equal.
But sum of these areas = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar (I) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC).

questions.question: Area of portion III =

questions.explanation: All of the properties of first three

questions.question: If {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, then which of the following is true?
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, so their corresponding parts are equal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = PQ, BC = QR and AC = PR.

questions.question: In the given figure, AP {tex}\perp{/tex} QR, PR > PQ and PS = PQ. Then
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}APQ and {tex}\triangle{/tex}APS, we have
PQ = PS [Given]
AP = AP [Common]
{tex}\angle{/tex}APQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}APS [Each 90°]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}APQ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}APS [By SAS congruency]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AQP = {tex}\angle{/tex}ASP [By C.P.C.T.]
or {tex}\angle{/tex}AQS = {tex}\angle{/tex}ASQ ...(1)
But, {tex}\angle{/tex}ASQ > {tex}\angle{/tex}ARS
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AQS > {tex}\angle{/tex}ARS [From (1)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AQR > {tex}\angle{/tex}ARQ
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AR > AQ [Side opposite to greater angle is longer]

questions.question: In the given figure, x and y are ________.
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD
AB = AC [Given]
BD = CD [Given]
AD = AD [Common]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD [By SSS congruency]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BDA = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDA (By C.P.C.T)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 70°
Similarly, x = 37°

questions.question: If the given triangles are congruent, then which of the following options is CORRECT?
questions.explanation: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}EDF

questions.question: In the given figure, AD is the median of BC and AB = AC. Then A and A are congruent by which criterion?
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD
AB = AC (Given)
BD= CD ({tex}\because{/tex} AD is the median)
AD = AD (Common)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD (By SSS congruency)
Also, AB = AC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD
and, CD = BD, AB = AC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD (by SAS)

questions.question: The congruence rule, by which the two triangles in the given figure are congruent is ________.
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS
PR = PS = 8 cm
{tex}\angle{/tex}RPQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}SPQ (Given)
PQ = PQ (Common)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS (By SAS congruency)

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, DE = DF, {tex}\angle{/tex}F = {tex}\angle{/tex}P and {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q. The two triangles are
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF, DE = DF. So, {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF is an isosceles.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}F = {tex}\angle{/tex}E
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}F = {tex}\angle{/tex}P and {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q
From (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\angle{/tex}P = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, {tex}\angle{/tex}P = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RQ = PR
So, {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is an isosceles.
Hence, {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR are isosceles but not necessarily congruent.

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, {tex}\angle{/tex}P = 75°, {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = 35° and {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 70° then
questions.explanation:
In any triangle the side opposite to the greater angle is longer.
Since, {tex}\angle{/tex}R > {tex}\angle{/tex}Q
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PQ > PR and {tex}\angle{/tex}Q < {tex}\angle{/tex}P
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PR < QR

questions.question: AD, BE and CP, the altitudes of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC are equal. Then
questions.explanation:
In right triangles BCE and CBF,
BC = CB [Common]
BE = CF [Given]
{tex}\angle{/tex}BEC = {tex}\angle{/tex}CFB [Each 90°]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCE = {tex}\triangle{/tex}CBF [By R.H.S. congruency]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = {tex}\angle{/tex}BCF [By C.P.C.T.]
and {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = AB [Sides opposite to equal angles of a {tex}\triangle{/tex} are equal]
Similarly, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAE
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC [By C.P.C.T.]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = BC [Sides opposite to equal angles of a {tex}\triangle{/tex} are equal]

questions.question: In the given figure, PQR is an equilateral triangle and QRST is a square. Then {tex}\angle{/tex}PSR = ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\angle{/tex}PRS = (90° + 60°) = 150°
RQ = PR ... (i) [Sides of equilateral {tex}\triangle{/tex}]
and RQ = RS ... (ii) [Sides of square]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} RP = RS [from (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RPS = {tex}\angle{/tex}RSP = x
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PSR, {tex}\angle{/tex}PRS + {tex}\angle{/tex}SPR + {tex}\angle{/tex}RSP = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 150° + x + x = 180° => 2x = 30° => x = 15°

questions.question: In the given figure, if PA = PD and PB = PC. Then {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAB is congruent to ________.
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PDC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAB
{tex}\angle{/tex}CPD = {tex}\angle{/tex}BPA (vertically opposite angles)
CP = BP (given)
PD = PA (given)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PDC = {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAB (By SAS congruency)

questions.question: If AB = DE, AC = DF, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 90° and BC = 5 cm, then EF is equal to _____.
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF
AB = DE (given)
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 90°
AC = DF (given)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF (By SAS congruency)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = EF = 5 cm (By C.P.C.T.)

questions.question: In the given figure, AB {tex}\perp{/tex} BE and FE {tex}\perp{/tex} BE. If AB = FE and BC = DE, then
questions.explanation: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}FEC

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, the medians AD, BE and CP passes through G. If BG = 6 units, then BE is ______.
questions.explanation:
We know that the centroid divides the median in the ratio 2 : 1.
{tex}\therefore B E=\frac{3}{2} B G=\frac{3}{2}(6){/tex} = 9 units

questions.question: If AD is bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}A and it is perpendicular to BC. Then {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is ________ triangle.
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD ({tex}\because{/tex} AD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A)
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC ({tex}\because{/tex} Each 90°)
AD = AD (common)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}ADS {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC (By ASA congruency)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AS = AC (by C.P.C.T.)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle.

questions.question:
Match the following.

Column-I Column-ll
(a) (i) SAS Rule
(b) (ii) RHS Rule
(c) (iv) SSS Rule
(d) (iv) AAS Rule
ABC is a right triangle such that AB = AC and bisector of angle C intersects the side AB at D. Then AC + AD = ________.
questions.explanation:
Given a right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, where AB = AC and CD is the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}C.

Let us draw DE {tex}\perp{/tex} BC.
In right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, AB = AC and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90°
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEC,
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}3 [Each 90°]
{tex}\angle{/tex}2 - {tex}\angle{/tex}1 [{tex}\because{/tex} CD is the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}C]
DC = DC [Common]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEC [By AAS congruency]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} DA = DE ...(1)
and AC = EC [By C.P.C.T.] ...(2)
Also, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, AB = AC {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}B ...(3) [Angles opposite to equal sides are equal]
Again, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180° [by angle sum property of triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 90° + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180° [From (3)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 90°{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 45°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BED,{tex}\angle{/tex}5 = 180° - ({tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}4)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}5 = 180°- (45° + 90°) = 180° - 135° = 45°
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}5 => DE = BE ...(4) [Sides opposite to equal angles are equal]
From (1) and (4),
DA = DE = BE ...(5)
{tex}\because{/tex} BC = EC + BE = AC + AD [from (2) and (5)]
Thus, AD + AC = BC

questions.question: In a square ABCD, the diagonals AC and BD bisects at O. Then {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB is
questions.explanation: Diagonals of a square are perpendicular bisectors, hence angle AOB = 90o  So triangle AOB is right-angled.

questions.question: ABCD is a rhombus. If {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 30o, then {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB is
questions.explanation: If angle ACB = 30o then Angle CAD = 30o (The diagonals bisect the angles in a Rhombus) and in triangle AOD, angle AOB = 90o diagonals perpendicular to each other; so by angle sum property of a triangle angle ADB = 60o.

questions.question: The diagonal AC and BD of quadrilateral ABCD are equal and are perpendicular bisector of each other then quadrilateral ABCD is a
questions.explanation: The triangles formed by the perpendicular bisectors are congruent to each other by SAS congruence. so the sides are equal hence it is a square.

questions.explanation: By SSS congruence condition; opposite sides are equal and common diagonal.

questions.question: The area of a quadrilateral whose diagonals measure 48 m and 32 m respectively and bisect each other at right angles is
questions.explanation:
According to the question,
Area of given quadrilateral = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Product of diagonals
= {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 48 {tex}\times{/tex} 32 
= 768 sq. m

questions.question: The diagonals AC and BD of a rectangle ABCD intersect each other at P. If {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 50o, then {tex}\angle{/tex}DPC =
questions.explanation:
Given,
ABCD is a rectangle

Diagonals AC & BD intersect each other at P
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 50o
∵ diagonals of rectangle bisect each other and are equal in length
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}PDC [alternate angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PDC= {tex}\angle{/tex}PCD = 50°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}DPC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DPC + {tex}\angle{/tex}PCD + {tex}\angle{/tex}PDC = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DPC + 50o + 50o = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DPC = 180o - 100o = 80o

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, E is the mid-point of median AD such that BE produced meets AC at F. If AC = 10.5 cm, then AF =
questions.explanation:
Given,
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
E is mid point of median AD

AC = 10.5 cm
Draw DM || EF
{tex}\because{/tex} E is mid point of AD so F is mid point of AM
AF = FM ...(i)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BFC
EF || DM
So, FM = MC ...(ii)
From (i) & (ii)
AF = MC ...(iii)
AC = AF + MC + FM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = AF + AF + AF From (i) (ii) &(iii)
AC = 3AF
AF = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} AC
AF = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10.5 = 3.5 cm

questions.question: If one angle of a parallelogram is 24o less than twice the smallest angle, then the measure of the largest angle of the parallelogram is
questions.explanation:
Let angles of parallelogram are {tex}\angle{/tex}A, {tex}\angle{/tex}B, {tex}\angle{/tex}C, {tex}\angle{/tex}D

Let smallest angle = {tex}\angle{/tex}A
Let largest angle = {tex}\angle{/tex}B
= {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}A – 24o ...(i)
{tex}\angle{/tex} A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180o [adjacent angle of parallelogram]
So, {tex}\angle{/tex}A + 2{tex}\angle{/tex}A -24o = 180o
= 3{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 180o + 24o = 204o
= {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\frac{204^{o}}{3}{/tex} = 68o
= {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 68o - 24o = 112o

questions.question: ABCD is a parallelogram and E and F are the centroids of triangles ABD and BCD respectively, then EF =
questions.explanation:
Given,
ABCD is a parallelogram
E & F are centroids of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD & {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD respectively
We know that diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other & centroid of a median divides it in 2 : 1
So, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD,
{tex}=\frac{A B}{E O}=\frac{2}{1}{/tex} or EO = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}AO & AE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} AO ...(i)

Similarly, FO = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}CO & CE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}CO ...(ii)
= AO = CO
= {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}AO = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}CO
From equations (i) & (ii)
EO = FO
EF = 2 FO ...(iii)
AE = CF…(iv)
From equation (i)
= AE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}AO = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}CO
=AE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} (CF + FO)
= AE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} (AE + FO) from equation ...(iv)
= AE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}AE + {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}DO
= AE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}AE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}FO
= {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}AE = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}FO
= {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}AE = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}EF from equation ...(iii)
= AE= EF

questions.question: If the diagonals of a rhombus are 18 cm and 24 cm respectively, then its side is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given,
ABCD is a rhombus

AC = 24 cm, BD = 18 cm
AB = BC = CD = DA [side of rhombus]
We know that diagonals of rhombus bisect each other at 90o
In right ∆ AOB
AB2 = BO2 + AO2
AB2 = 122 + 92 = 144 + 81 = 225
AB = {tex}\sqrt{225}{/tex} = 15 cm
Side of rhombus = 15 cm

questions.question: ABCD is a parallelogram in which diagonal AC bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD. If {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 35o, then {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC =
questions.explanation:
Given,
ABCD is a parallelogram
Diagonal AC bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD

{tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 35o
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180o ...(i) [ angle sum property of quadrilateral]
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 35o = 70o
Putting value of ∠A in equation (i)
70o + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180o - 70o = 110o
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 110o

questions.question: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 30o, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 40o and {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 110o. The angles of the triangle formed by joining the mid-points of the sides of this triangle are
questions.explanation:
Given,
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC

{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 30°,{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 40°,{tex}\angle{/tex}C = 110o
In figure, BDEF, DCEF, DEAF are parallelograms so,
∠B = ∠E = 40o [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B & {tex}\angle{/tex}E are opposite angles of parallelogram BDEF]
∠C = ∠F = 110o [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} & {tex}\angle{/tex}F are opposite angles of parallelogram DCEF]
∠A = ∠D = 30o [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A & {tex}\angle{/tex}D are opposite angles of parallelogram DEAF]
Hence, {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 30o
{tex}\angle{/tex}E = 40o
{tex}\angle{/tex}F = 110o

questions.question: ABCD is a trapezium in which AB || DC. M and N are the mid-points of AD and BC respectively. If AB = 12 cm, MN = 14 cm, then CD =
questions.explanation:
Given,

ABCD is a trapezium
AB || DC
M, N are mid points of AD & BC
AB = 12 cm, MN = 14 cm
{tex}\because{/tex}  AB || MN || CD [M, N are mid points of AD &BC]
MP = NP
By mid point theorem,
MP = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}CD and NP = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}AB
{tex}\therefore{/tex} MN = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}(AB + CD)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} (12 + CD)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CD = 28 - 12 = 16 cm

questions.question: Diagonals of a quadrilateral ABCD bisect each other. If {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 45o, then {tex}\angle{/tex}B =
questions.explanation:
Given,

ABCD is a quadrilateral
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 45o,
{tex}\because{/tex} diagonals of quadrilateral bisects each other hence ABCD is a parallelogram,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 45o + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180o - 45o = 135o

questions.question: P is the mid-point of side BC of a parallelogram ABCD such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BAP = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAP. If AD = 10 cm, then CD =
questions.explanation:
Given,
ABCD is a parallelogram

P is mid point of side BC
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAP = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAP
AD = 10cm
{tex}\because{/tex} AD || BC
{tex}\angle{/tex}DAP = {tex}\angle{/tex}APB [alternate angles]
{tex}\angle{/tex}DAP = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAP
AB = BP (side opposite to equal angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BP = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} BC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} BC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 5 cm
AB = CD = 5cm (sides of parallelogram)
Hence, CD = 5 cm

questions.question: In which of the following figures are the diagonals equal?
questions.explanation: Rectangle is the correct answer. As we know that from all the quadrilaterals given in other options, diagonals of a rectangle are equal.

questions.question: The lengths of the diagonals of a rhombus are 16 cm and 12 cm. The length of each side of the rhombus is
questions.explanation:
Let us assume a rhombus ABCD where,
AB = BC = CD = DA
Now, in triangle OBC by using Pythagoras theorem we get:
BC2 = OB2 + OC2
BC2 = 62 + 82
BC2 = 36 + 64
BC2 = 100
BC = {tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex}
BC = 10 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = BC = CD = DA = 10 cm

questions.question: A diagonal of a rectangle is inclined to one side of the rectangle at 35o. The acute angle between the diagonals is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\angle{/tex}OAD = 90o - ({tex}\angle{/tex}OAB)
= 90o - 35o = 55o.
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}ODA = {tex}\angle{/tex}OAD = 55o  [{tex}\because{/tex} OA = OD since diagonals of a rectangles are equal and bisect each other].
{tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = 180o - ({tex}\angle{/tex}OAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}ODA)
= 180o - (55o + 55o) = 70o.

questions.question: The figure formed by joining the mid-points of the adjacent sides of a rhombus is a
questions.explanation:
rectangle

Let ABCD be a rhombus and P,Q,R and S be the mid-points of sides AB, BC, CD and DA respectively.
In △ABD and △BDC we have
SP ∥ BD and SP = {tex}\frac12{/tex}BD ..... (1) [By mid-point theorem]
RQ ∥ BD and RQ = {tex}\frac12{/tex}BD ..... (2) [By mid-point theorem]
From (1) and (2) we get,
SP ∥ RQ
PQRS is a parallelogram
As diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other at right angles.
∴ AC⊥BD
Since, SP ∥ BD, PQ ∥ AC and AC⊥BD
∴ SP⊥PQ
∴ ∠QPS = 90o
∴ PQRS is a rectangle.

questions.question: The two diagonals are equal in a
questions.explanation:
ABCD is a rectangle with AC and BD as its diagonals.
ABCD is a rectangle
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90o, AD = BC
AD || BC and AB is a transversal
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 90o
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAC
AB = BA
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B
AD = BC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAC(SAS)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}BD = AC (c.p.c.t)
Hence, the diagonals of a rectangle are equal.

questions.question: Measure of {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB is:
questions.explanation:
ABCD is a rhombus and adjacent angles of a rhombus are supplementary.
Thus, {tex}\angle{/tex}CDA + {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 120o + {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = 180o - 120o = 60o

questions.question: Measure of {tex}\angle{/tex}CDO and {tex}\angle{/tex}DCO are ________ and ________ respectively.
questions.explanation:
Diagonal of a rhombus bisects the angles is passing from.
So, {tex}\angle{/tex}CDO = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CDA
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (120)o = 60o
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADC + {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = 180o
120o + {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = 180o - 120o = 60o
{tex}\angle{/tex} DCO = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (60o) = 30o

questions.explanation: Diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular bisectors.

Since, {tex}\angle{/tex}CDO > {tex}\angle{/tex}DCO
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CO > DO (side opposite to greater angle is greater.)
